title: How do I allow my program to edit its configuration files in /usr/local/etc?
text: I am writing a Python program that reads some sensors using a Raspberry Pi and the pigpio library and publishes that data to a MODBUS TCP server. I have packaged this Python script as a binary executable using PyInstaller. My program is packaged with a few files DataLoggerProgram DataLoggerProgram.desktop install.sh killallpigpiod.sh The roles of the program file and the desktop file are exactly as would be expected. One executes the script and the other acts as a shortcut for user and autostart. The killallpigpiod.sh script is a way I am allowing the program to run sudo killall pigpiod without giving it sudo access to the entire killall command. Once the program is launched, it creates three files which store configuration data (typically one time setup by the user and never touched again). I am trying to follow "best practice", so my understanding is that the binary should go in /usr/local/bin with configuration files in /usr/local/etc/DataLoggerProgram and the desktop entry in /usr/local/share/applications (as well as ~/.config/autostart for autostart). I have my install.sh script detailed below: #!/bin/bash #Allow user to run certain sudo command "killall pigpiod", "pigpiod", and "shutdown" echo "$USER ALL=NOPASSWD:/usr/local/bin/killallpigpiod.sh, /usr/local/bin/pigpiod, /usr/sbin/shutdown" | (sudo su -c 'EDITOR="tee" visudo -f /etc/sudoers.d/dataloggerprogram') #Ensure needed directories exist mkdir ~/.config/autostart sudo mkdir /usr/local/bin sudo mkdir /usr/local/etc/DataLoggerProgram sudo mkdir /usr/local/share/applications #Copy files to appropriate locations sudo cp killallpigpiod.sh /usr/local/bin sudo cp DataLoggerProgram /usr/local/bin sudo cp DataLoggerProgram.desktop /usr/local/share/applications #remove the files from the install folder rm DataLoggerProgram.desktop rm DataLoggerProgram rm killallpigpiod.sh #Set Permissions sudo chown root:root /usr/local/bin/DataLoggerProgram /usr/local/bin/killallpigpiod.sh /usr/local/share/applications/DataLoggerProgram.desktop sudo chmod 755 /usr/local/bin/DataLoggerProgram /usr/local/bin/killallpigpiod.sh sudo chmod 644 /usr/local/share/applications/DataLoggerProgram.desktop #Add additional desktop shortcuts cp /usr/local/share/applications/DataLoggerProgram.desktop ~/.config/autostart cp /usr/local/share/applications/DataLoggerProgram.desktop ~/Desktop #Install libcap sudo apt install libcap2-bin #Allow binary to bind to "privilaged" network ports (502) sudo setcap 'cap_net_bind_service=+ep' /usr/local/bin/DataLoggerProgram #Install PIGPIO Dameon sudo apt install python-setuptools python3-setuptools wget https://github.com/joan2937/pigpio/archive/master.zip unzip master.zip cd pigpio-master make sudo make install #Cleanup cd ../ rm install.sh The root issue I am running into is that my Python program (which I do not wish to run as root) cannot access the /usr/local/etc/DataLoggerProgram folder to create its configuration files. How are permissions handled for this. Should I just chmod the DataLoggerProgram folder to allow non-privileged users to edit it? Should I put the configuration files in a different location?

title: Foundry Local SDK ignores CUDAExecutionProvider, forces CPUExecutionProvider on Windows
text: I am developing a local RAG application using foundry-local-sdk (v1.2.3) on Windows 11 with an NVIDIA RTX 5070 GPU. I am unable to get the SDK to utilize my GPU for inference, as it consistently falls back to CPUExecutionProvider despite my explicit configuration. Environment: OS: Windows 11 Hardware: NVIDIA RTX 5070 SDK Package: foundry-local-sdk-winml (installed via pip) Runtime: Python 3.14 (venv) The Issue: When initializing the FoundryLocalManager with CUDAExecutionProvider settings, the model fails to load with the following error: Plaintext System.InvalidOperationException: Cannot load model 'Phi-3.5-mini-instruct-cuda-gpu:2': it requires the 'CUDAExecutionProvider' execution provider, which is not available. Available EPs: [CPUExecutionProvider]. Steps I have taken: I have installed onnxruntime-genai-cuda and onnxruntime-genai-directml . I configured the Configuration object as follows: Python config = Configuration( app_name="my-app", additional_settings={ "execution_provider": "CUDAExecutionProvider", "device": "gpu" } ) I verified that the model variant Phi-3.5-mini-instruct-cuda-gpu:2 exists and is cached. I suspect that the foundry-local-sdk-winml package is bundled with a winml specific core runtime that is hardcoded to use CPUExecutionProvider , effectively ignoring custom provider settings in Configuration . My Question: Is there a way to force foundry-local-sdk to use CUDAExecutionProvider when using the winml variant, or is this SDK package strictly limited to CPU-only execution? If the latter, what is the recommended way to utilize GPU acceleration for inference with models managed by this SDK (e.g., extracting the model path and using onnxruntime-genai directly)? Any insights into whether I should switch to a non-winml package (if one exists) or how to properly register the CUDA provider within the current SDK environment would be greatly appreciated. "Note: I am aware of the winml package limitations, but I wanted to verify if there's any workaround before completely bypassing the SDK."

title: Passed timeout not overriding default timeout in GET/POST request
text: I'm working with Playwright for Python for a simple web scraping project. I heavily use APIRequestContext in my process and I notice that even though I passed timeout to the timeout argument, still I see 30000ms timeout. It seems that the timeout I'm passing is not overriding the default timeout. Just to demonstrate: import os from playwright.async_api import async_playwright timeout = int(os.getenv('TIMEOUT', 180000)) pw = await async_playwright().start() context = await browser.new_context() context.set_default_timeout(timeout) page = await context.new_page() # Send GET/POST request request = page.context.request response = await request.post( url, params=params, timeout=timeout, headers=headers ) Looking at the documentation for request timeout , the default timeout is 30000ms. My understanding is, when I set the timeout to 180000 or whatever ever value, it should overwrite the default timeout. After checking at the logs, I found out the that the value of timeout variable was really 180000. But I got an error with APIRequestContext.post: Timeout 30000ms exceeded So, am I missing something here? In my environment, I'm currently using: Python 3.11.0rc1 Playwright 1.55.0 Ubuntu 22.04.3 LTS

title: GCS cross-region issue - Upload hangs indefinitely when uploading from a node in `Germany` to a gcp bucket in `asia-south1`
text: google-cloud-storage upload_from_filename hangs indefinitely despite timeout and retry . The reason I have mentioned timeout and retry is because many people over the internet where talking about how "chunk uploading" can hang indefinitely when using google-cloud-storage without retry and timeout . I have a Python service that records LiveKit video/audio to a local MP4 file, then uploads it to Google Cloud Storage. The upload intermittently hangs forever — the process never returns from upload_from_filename , never raises, and never times out. The GCSUploader : from google.cloud import storage from google.cloud.storage.retry import DEFAULT_RETRY _UPLOAD_TIMEOUT = 120.0 _UPLOAD_RETRY = DEFAULT_RETRY.with_deadline(600.0) class GCSUploader: def __init__(self, bucket: str): self._client = storage.Client() self._bucket = self._client.bucket(bucket) def _upload_sync(self, local_path: str, blob_name: str): blob = self._bucket.blob(blob_name) self._logger.info( "uploading to GCS", extra={ "blob": blob_name, "size_bytes": file_size, "id": recording_id, }, ) blob.upload_from_filename( local_path, content_type="video/mp4", timeout=_UPLOAD_TIMEOUT, # per-chunk timeout retry=_UPLOAD_RETRY, # retry deadline 600s ) async def upload(self, local_path: str, blob_name: str): loop = asyncio.get_event_loop() await loop.run_in_executor(None, self._upload_sync, local_path, blob_name) The recorder , if this is killed due to timeout I retry upload for the complete file again: async def upload_with_timeout(uploader, path, blob_name): return await asyncio.wait_for( uploader.upload(path, blob_name), timeout=600.0, # whole-upload hard limit ) What happens is that I see "uploading to GCS" in the logs but then all the events after that just stop. I haven't added other parts of the code since the post will get huge Can someone please help me? If something is unclear please ask I'll provide extra details.

title: FastAPI returning "Out of range float values are not JSON compliant" with Pandas NaN values
text: I am building a FastAPI endpoint that reads data, processes it into a Pandas DataFrame, and returns it as a JSON response. However, my dataset contains missing data ( NaN ). When I convert the DataFrame to a dictionary and return it, FastAPI/Pydantic throws a 500 Internal Server Error because standard JSON does not support NaN . import pandas as pd import numpy as np from fastapi import FastAPI app = FastAPI() @app.get("/data") def get_data(): df = pd.DataFrame({ "id": [1, 2, 3], "value": [10.5, np.nan, 30.2] }) # This causes the error because of np.nan return df.to_dict(orient="records") I have tried: Using df.fillna(None) , but Pandas often ignores None in float columns and keeps it as NaN . df.replace({np.nan: None}) , which works for the JSON serialization, but it forces my numeric columns to become object dtypes, which breaks downstream vectorized calculations I need to do before returning the data. I know orjson supports NaN serialization, but I would prefer to stick to FastAPI's default responses if possible. What is the most efficient, Pandas-native way to handle NaN to null conversion specifically for API JSON responses without mutating my column datatypes to object ?

title: FastAPI event loop blocked by Scikit-Learn Joblib thread-pool exhaustion during concurrent async inference
text: I am deploying a Random Forest model using FastAPI. Because Scikit-Learn models are synchronous and CPU-bound, I am offloading the inference to asyncio.get_running_loop().run_in_executor() to prevent blocking the main FastAPI event loop. However, under high concurrency (e.g., 50+ simultaneous requests), the entire FastAPI event loop freezes. I have traced the issue down to a thread-pool collision between asyncio 's ThreadPoolExecutor and Scikit-Learn's internal joblib (OpenMP) threading. import asyncio from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor import joblib import numpy as np from fastapi import FastAPI from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier app = FastAPI() # Dummy model pre-trained with n_jobs=-1 (uses all cores) model = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, n_jobs=-1) model.fit(np.random.rand(100, 10), np.random.randint(0, 2, 100)) # Dedicated executor for ML inference ml_executor = ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=4) def predict_sync(data): # Even with an external executor, joblib spawns its own threads here return model.predict(data) @app.post("/predict") async def predict_async(): loop = asyncio.get_running_loop() dummy_data = np.random.rand(1, 10) # Offloading to prevent blocking the event loop prediction = await loop.run_in_executor(ml_executor, predict_sync, dummy_data) return {"prediction": prediction.tolist()} When multiple requests hit /predict simultaneously, the ThreadPoolExecutor workers execute predict_sync . Inside predict_sync , Scikit-Learn attempts to use joblib to spawn OpenMP threads across all CPU cores ( n_jobs=-1 ). This causes massive thread contention and context-switching overhead, effectively starving the OS and locking up the ASGI server (Uvicorn) entirely. I have tried: Changing the Joblib backend: Wrapping the prediction in with joblib.parallel_backend('loky'): prevents the thread collision, but the serialization overhead of spawning Loky processes for single-row inference makes the API unacceptably slow (adding ~200ms latency per request). Setting n_jobs=1 : If I restrict the model to n_jobs=1 during inference, the event loop survives, but I lose the parallelization benefits of the Random Forest, increasing single-request latency. Using ProcessPoolExecutor: Passing the whole model to a ProcessPoolExecutor eats up too much RAM because the model is duplicated across processes rather than sharing memory. Is there a known, production-grade pattern to isolate Scikit-Learn's OpenMP thread pool from ASGI/Asyncio thread pools in Python without incurring the massive IPC (Inter-Process Communication) overhead of process pools for single-row, real-time API inference?

title: How do I get a value from a tkinter scale/slider whilst when it is in a function?
text: It is a very long program so I've only added the relevant parts of my code below. title: How to make my voxel rasteriser run faster?
text: I'm making a terrain generator with a voxel rasteriser that I coded but it is very slow even with just 25 blocks. I think it may be a turtle issue but are there any other ways I could optimise? The way the code works is in the bottom Blockquote, it calculates the angle the mouse has to the centre of the screen before moving the mouse back there, and sends that angle together with voxel coordinates to the first Blockquote. the first Blockquote is just a simple graphics pipeline (without z-buffering) and the "block()" function creates all the tris neccesary to construct the block, and Turtle does all the drawing. #=============================================================================== #= Logic Layer =# #=============================================================================== #libraries---------------------------------------------------------------------- from math import sin, cos, acos from numpy import matmul, array, float64 from turtle import Turtle, Screen from time import sleep s = Screen() s.delay(0) s.tracer(0,0) s.screensize(200,200) t = Turtle() t.speed(0) t.penup() t.shape("square") t.shapesize(0.5,0.5) t.ht() #translation-------------------------------------------------------------------- def translateX(pos,distance): pos[0] += distance return pos def translateY(pos,distance): pos[1] += distance return pos def translateZ(pos,distance): pos[2] += distance return pos #rotation----------------------------------------------------------------------- def rotateX(pos, cosT, sinT): A = array(pos[1:]) B = array([[cosT, -sinT], [sinT, cosT]]) pos[1:] = map(float, matmul(A,B)) return pos def rotateY(pos, cosT, sinT): A = array([pos[0],pos[2]]) B = array([[cosT, -sinT], [sinT, cosT]]) pos[0],pos[2] = map(float, matmul(A,B)) return pos #projection--------------------------------------------------------------------- def project(pos, f=256): xProj = int(f*pos[0] / (f+pos[2])) yProj = int(f*pos[1] / (f+pos[2])) return xProj, yProj #rasterisation------------------------------------------------------------------ def rasterise(x0,y0, x1,y1, x2,y2, colour): if (x1-x0)*(y2-y0) - (y1-y0)*(x2-x0) >= 0: return t.color(colour) minX = min(x0,x1,x2) minY = min(y0,y1,y2) maxX = max(x0,x1,x2) + 1 maxY = max(y0,y1,y2) + 1 dy01 = y1-y0 dx01 = x1-x0 dy12 = y2-y1 dx12 = x2-x1 dy20 = y0-y2 dx20 = x0-x2 OLDedge01 = dy01*(minX-x0) - (minY-y0)*dx01 OLDedge12 = dy12*(minX-x1) - (minY-y1)*dx12 OLDedge20 = dy20*(minX-x2) - (minY-y2)*dx20 for y in range(minY,maxY): edge01 = OLDedge01 edge12 = OLDedge12 edge20 = OLDedge20 for x in range(minX,maxX): if edge01 >= 0 and edge12 >= 0 and edge20 >= 0: t.goto(x*10,y*10) t.stamp() edge01 += dy01 edge12 += dy12 edge20 += dy20 OLDedge01 -= dx01 OLDedge12 -= dx12 OLDedge20 -= dx20 #------------------------------------------------------------------------------- def block(x,y,z, cosyaw,sinyaw, cospitch,sinpitch, blockSize=20): x *= blockSize y *= blockSize z *= blockSize corner0 = [x, y, z] corner1 = [x, y+blockSize, z] corner2 = [x+blockSize, corner1[1], z] corner3 = [corner2[0], y, z] corner4 = [x, y, z+blockSize] corner5 = [x, corner1[1], corner4[2]] corner6 = [corner2[0], corner1[1], corner4[2]] corner7 = [corner2[0], y, corner4[2]] if acos(cosyaw) != 0: corner0 = rotateY(corner0, cosyaw,sinyaw) corner1 = rotateY(corner1, cosyaw,sinyaw) corner2 = rotateY(corner2, cosyaw,sinyaw) corner3 = rotateY(corner3, cosyaw,sinyaw) corner4 = rotateY(corner4, cosyaw,sinyaw) corner5 = rotateY(corner5, cosyaw,sinyaw) corner6 = rotateY(corner6, cosyaw,sinyaw) corner7 = rotateY(corner7, cosyaw,sinyaw) if acos(cospitch) != 0: corner0 = rotateX(corner0, cospitch,sinpitch) corner1 = rotateX(corner1, cospitch,sinpitch) corner2 = rotateX(corner2, cospitch,sinpitch) corner3 = rotateX(corner3, cospitch,sinpitch) corner4 = rotateX(corner4, cospitch,sinpitch) corner5 = rotateX(corner5, cospitch,sinpitch) corner6 = rotateX(corner6, cospitch,sinpitch) corner7 = rotateX(corner7, cospitch,sinpitch) corner0x, corner0y = project(corner0) corner1x, corner1y = project(corner1) corner2x, corner2y = project(corner2) corner3x, corner3y = project(corner3) corner4x, corner4y = project(corner4) corner5x, corner5y = project(corner5) corner6x, corner6y = project(corner6) corner7x, corner7y = project(corner7) rasterise(corner0x,corner0y,corner1x,corner1y,corner2x,corner2y,"red") rasterise(corner0x,corner0y,corner2x,corner2y,corner3x,corner3y,"red") rasterise(corner4x,corner4y,corner5x,corner5y,corner1x,corner1y,"orange") rasterise(corner4x,corner4y,corner1x,corner1y,corner0x,corner0y,"orange") rasterise(corner3x,corner3y,corner2x,corner2y,corner6x,corner6y,"yellow") rasterise(corner3x,corner3y,corner6x,corner6y,corner7x,corner7y,"yellow") rasterise(corner5x,corner5y,corner4x,corner4y,corner7x,corner7y,"green") rasterise(corner5x,corner5y,corner7x,corner7y,corner6x,corner6y,"green") rasterise(corner4x,corner4y,corner0x,corner0y,corner3x,corner3y,"pink") rasterise(corner4x,corner4y,corner3x,corner3y,corner7x,corner7y,"pink") rasterise(corner1x,corner1y,corner5x,corner5y,corner6x,corner6y,"purple") rasterise(corner1x,corner1y,corner6x,corner6y,corner2x,corner2y,"purple") import pyautogui from keyboard import is_pressed import turtle from math import sin, cos from NEAlogicLayer import * screenWidth, screenHeight = pyautogui.size() midX, midY = screenWidth//2, screenHeight//2 escPressed = 0 yaw = 0 pitch = 0 sensitivity = 0.003 def frame(): global yaw global pitch if is_pressed("esc"): s.bye() return dx = pyautogui.position().x - midX dy = pyautogui.position().y - midY yaw += dx * sensitivity pitch += dy * sensitivity cosyaw, sinyaw = cos(yaw), sin(yaw) cospitch, sinpitch = cos(pitch), sin(pitch) for Y in range(-2,3): for X in range(-2,3): block(X,0,Y, cosyaw,sinyaw, cospitch,sinpitch) #pyautogui.moveTo(midX,midY) s.update() s.ontimer(frame,16) t.clear() frame() s.mainloop()

title: How do I clear Warning Message When Using SHM C to Python
text: I have a simple app to transfer a struct from C to Python. It works, but, I get a warning message when the Python code exits. The warning changes if I exit C before Python, as the SHM is killed in C and my_shared_memory no longer exits. The resource tracker part of the warning still exists. I can just ignore the warning, but, I worry that this may be hiding some omission on my part that will be a bigger problem later. If I exit C first, How do I prevent this warning? Warning Message: > /usr/lib/python3.10/multiprocessing/resource_tracker.py:224: UserWarning: resource_tracker: There appear to be 1 leaked shared_memory objects to clean up at shutdown warnings.warn('resource_tracker: There appear to be %d ' C Code: /////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////// // includes #include #include #include #include // uint32_t #include #include #include /////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////// // defines #define SHM_NAME "my_shared_memory" #define SHM_SIZE 1024 /////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////// // typedefs typedef struct shm_t { uint32_t count; int32_t lenc; float volts; char buf[32]; } shm_t; /////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////// // main int main() { static shm_t shm_struct; // 1. Create the shared memory object int shm_fd = shm_open(SHM_NAME, O_CREAT | O_RDWR, 0666); if (shm_fd == -1) { perror("shm_open failed"); return 1; } // 2. Configure the size of the shared memory segment if (ftruncate(shm_fd, SHM_SIZE) == -1) { perror("ftruncate failed"); return 1; } // 3. Map the shared memory segment into the process address space shm_t *ptr = (shm_t *)mmap(0, SHM_SIZE, PROT_READ | PROT_WRITE, MAP_SHARED, shm_fd, 0); if (ptr == MAP_FAILED) { perror("mmap failed"); return 1; } // 4. Write data to the shared memory ptr->count = 100; ptr->lenc = -2354; ptr->volts = 3.345; strncpy(ptr->buf, "Hello from C process!", 32); printf("C wrote to shared memory\n"); // Keep running so Python can read before the memory is destroyed printf("Press Enter to clean up and exit: "); getchar(); printf("\n"); // 5. Cleanup munmap(ptr, SHM_SIZE); close(shm_fd); shm_unlink(SHM_NAME); return 0; } Python Code: #!/usr/bin/python import time from ctypes import Structure, c_int32, c_uint32, c_float, c_char from multiprocessing import shared_memory import os SHM_NAME = "/my_shared_memory" SHM_SIZE = 1024 class SharedData(Structure): _fields_ = [ ("count", c_uint32), ("lenc", c_uint32), ("volts", c_float), ("message", c_char * 32) ] def main(): shm_name = "my_shared_memory" # Note: Python drops the leading '/' print("Python: Waiting for C to create shared memory...") shm = None while not shm: try: shm = shared_memory.SharedMemory(name=shm_name) except FileNotFoundError: time.sleep(0.5) try: # Map the Python struct directly to the shared memory buffer ptr = SharedData.from_buffer(shm.buf) # print the data print(f'Read:') print(f' Count {ptr.count}') print(f' Lenc {ptr.lenc}') print(f' volts {ptr.volts}') print(f' Message {ptr.message.decode("utf-8")}') finally: # Close connection to the block (C handles the unlinking/deletion) x = input('Press enter:') ptr = None if (shm): shm.close() if __name__ == "__main__": main()

title: Full string not added to dictionary in loop
text: I am running into an issue where my dictionary is not picking up full strings attached to variables, but instead just the first letter. import numpy as np import random trials = 5 d = {'trial': np.empty([trials]), 'fruit': np.empty([trials], dtype = str), 'vegetable': np.empty([trials], dtype = str)} fruits = ['apple', 'orange', 'banana'] vegetables = ['carrot', 'squash', 'sprout'] for i in range(trials): fruit = random.choice(fruits) vegetable = random.choice(vegetables) # trial data d['trial'][i] = i d['fruit'][i] = fruit d['vegetable'][i] = vegetable print(d) This code just takes the first letter of the fruit/vegetable to add to my dictionary. This is messing with me, as I need to record "lvf" and "lower" in terms of visual fields in my real experiment, but they both appear as "l" in my dictionary. I have tried adding list() around my variables, but that didn’t help. For example: fruit = random.choice(list(fruits))

title: How can I prevent users from enrolling with a face shown over a video call in a Flutter + Python face recognition system?
text: I'm developing a workspace application with a Face Recognition module for employee enrollment. Tech stack: Frontend: Flutter AI Server: Python Face detection and face recognition are already implemented. Liveness detection is also implemented. The problem is that some users are able to pass the liveness check by showing another person's face through a video call (e.g., FaceTime, WhatsApp, Zoom, etc.) during enrollment. I'm looking for techniques or algorithms that can better distinguish: A real person physically in front of the camera. A face displayed on another device's screen during a live video call. I'm interested in approaches such as: Advanced anti-spoofing methods. Detection of screen reflections, moiré patterns, or display artifacts. Challenge-response techniques. Depth estimation. Any Flutter- or Python-compatible libraries or models. Has anyone dealt with this problem? What approaches are commonly used in production systems to reduce or prevent this type of attack?

title: How can I boxplot values that wrap around using matplotlib?
text: I want to show the distribution of angle estimations using a boxplot. The problem is that angles wrap around (I chose +-180° as the wrapping point, but the same applies to 0/360°). Matplotlibs boxplot method interprets the angles as linear values, which leads to wrong results in some edge cases. Take the following example (scatter plot for reference). Some boxes have extremely long whiskers because when calculating the quantiles, matplotlib is not ware that there's a "shorter path" across the 180° boundary. For example at 150°, it really is a tight cluster of measurements around +-180°, but it appears as a huge span across the entire data range. I can get away with using the 1.5*IQR convention, which marks the far off values as outliers. For the data that I'm currently dealing with, that would work. However I'm still interested if theres a proper solution to this problem. I'm using pandas and numpy to read and prepare the data.

title: GCP Agent Platform pipelines ModelGetOp issue
text: I am trying to upload a trained model that is already in the Model Registry into GCP Agent pipeline. The pipeline is simple: @kfp.dsl.pipeline( name=DISPLAY_NAME, pipeline_root=BUCKET_URI, ) def pipeline(PROJECT_ID: str): model_task = ModelGetOp( model_name='projects/80514224188/locations/us-central1/models/1800006515679555824', project=PROJECT_ID, location=GCP_REGION, ) endpoint_op = EndpointCreateOp( project=PROJECT_ID, location=GCP_REGION, display_name=DISPLAY_NAME, ) ModelDeployOp( model=model_task.outputs["model"], endpoint=endpoint_op.outputs["endpoint"], dedicated_resources_min_replica_count=1, dedicated_resources_max_replica_count=1, dedicated_resources_machine_type=MACHINE_TYPE, ) The model unique name below is fake, but the real model has exactly the same string structure. All three string input parameters to ModelGetOp are successfully passed into the pipeline (I can see them as parameters in the pipeline step). I can also successfully get the model outside the pipeline like this: model = aiplatform.Model('projects/80514224188/locations/us-central1/models/180000515679821824') Pipeline fails at the ModelGetOp with following error: 400, Request contains an invalid argument. There is nothing more in the logs. The endpoint is created successfully too. Does anyone know what am I doing wrong?

title: DB Browser for SQLite returns 0 rows for sqlite_master after opening a databasemessage in a Python UI automation test?
text: I have a Python UI automation test that verifies a transient status message after a long-press action. The transient is displayed for a very short time and is often missed when I read it after the action completes. Current approach: Perform long press. Read transient text. Verify expected message. This is flaky because the transient disappears before it is read. I considered: Polling for the transient after the action. Running a background thread that continuously captures transient text while the action is executing. Verifying a durable UI state instead of the transient. What is the recommended approach for making this type of test reliable while still verifying that the transient actually appeared? Python version: 3.7 transient_texts = [] am_screen.save_preset() try: transient_texts.append(TransientStatusBar().get_transient_text()) except LookupError: pass assert "Preset saved" in transient_texts

It used to work with Chrome 108.0.5359.124, but it stopped working when I updated to 149.0.7827.155. I'm using Selenium 4.46.0. I've included the Selenium log below which suggests checking the ChromeDriver verbose log. I tried to get ChromeDriver to write logs directly to chrome.log, but it's always blank. - [ ] langchain-perplexity
- [ ] langchain-qdrant
- [ ] langchain-xai
- [ ] Other / not sure / general

### Related Issues / PRs

* #38840 reports the same bug class (in-place mutation of a caller dict on the Responses route) in langchain-perplexity.
* #38868 was this same report; it was auto-closed because the package checkbox failed to register on submit, so refiling with the package selected.

### Reproduction Steps / Example Code (Python)

```python
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI

# Caller-owned options dict, passed by reference via model_kwargs.
my_text_opts = {"verbosity": "low"}

llm = ChatOpenAI(
    model="gpt-4.1",
    api_key="test",
    use_responses_api=True,
    model_kwargs={"text": my_text_opts},
)

print("caller dict BEFORE any call:        ", my_text_opts)

# Call 1: a JSON mode / structured output request (what with_structured_output
# with method="json_mode" binds: response_format={"type": "json_object"}).
# Offline: only builds the request payload, never touches the network.
payload1 = llm._get_request_payload(
    "give me json", response_format={"type": "json_object"}
)
print("payload1['text'] (expected format): ", payload1["text"])

print("caller dict AFTER structured call:  ", my_text_opts)
print("llm.model_kwargs AFTER:             ", llm.model_kwargs)

# Call 2: a PLAIN call, no response_format anywhere.
payload2 = llm._get_request_payload("just chat normally")
print("payload2['text'] (plain call!):     ", payload2["text"])

assert "format" not in my_text_opts, "caller's dict was mutated in place"
```

### Error Message and Stack Trace (if applicable)

```shell
Observed output (langchain-openai 1.3.5 and current master):

caller dict BEFORE any call:         {'verbosity': 'low'}
payload1['text'] (expected format):  {'verbosity': 'low', 'format': {'type': 'json_object'}}
caller dict AFTER structured call:   {'verbosity': 'low', 'format': {'type': 'json_object'}}
llm.model_kwargs AFTER:              {'text': {'verbosity': 'low', 'format': {'type': 'json_object'}}}
payload2['text'] (plain call!):      {'verbosity': 'low', 'format': {'type': 'json_object'}}
AssertionError: caller's dict was mutated in place
```

### Description

With use_responses_api=True, `_construct_responses_api_payload` writes the response format and verbosity straight into `payload["text"]`:

* `payload["text"]["format"] = {"type": "json_object"}` (JSON mode)
* `payload["text"]["format"] = format_value` (json_schema)
* `payload["text"]["verbosity"] = verbosity`

(`libs/partners/openai/langchain_openai/chat_models/base.py`, around lines 4356-4383 on current master.)

The problem is that `payload` is built by shallow-merging `self.model_kwargs` (and per-call kwargs), so `payload["text"]` is the very same dict object the caller passed as `model_kwargs={"text": {...}}`. Those writes therefore land in the instance's `model_kwargs` and in the caller's own dict.

What I expect: request payload construction should not modify the model instance or the caller's inputs. Each call's `text.format` should apply to that call only.

What happens instead: after one JSON mode or structured output call, `model_kwargs["text"]` permanently contains `format: {"type": "json_object"}`, so every later plain call on the same instance is silently forced into JSON mode (see payload2 in the repro, which never asked for any response format). The caller's dict is also corrupted, so sharing one options dict across two ChatOpenAI instances spreads the pollution.

The fix looks small: copy the `text` dict once during payload construction before writing into it, mirroring what was done for the same bug class in langchain-perplexity (#38840). I have the fix and a regression test ready. I'd like to work on this, could you assign it to me?

### System Info

System Information
------------------
> OS:  Windows
> OS Version:  10.0.26200
> Python Version:  3.11.0 (main, Oct 24 2022, 18:26:48) [MSC v.1933 64 bit (AMD64)]

Package Information
-------------------
> langchain_core: 1.4.9
> langchain: 1.3.13
> langsmith: 0.10.5
> langchain_openai: 1.3.5
> langgraph_sdk: 0.4.2

Also reproduced against current master (commit 7bf8fe2), where the same in-place writes are present in _construct_responses_api_payload.

title: ChatAnthropic streaming drops thinking field from empty thinking blocks (signature_delta-only), replay fails with 400 thinking.thinking: Field required
text:
### Submission checklist

- [x] This is a bug, not a usage question.
- [x] I added a clear and descriptive title that summarizes this issue.
- [x] I used the GitHub search to find a similar question and didn't find it.
- [x] I am sure that this is a bug in LangChain rather than my code.
- [x] The bug is not resolved by updating to the latest stable version of LangChain (or the specific integration package).
- [x] This is not related to the langchain-community package.
- [x] I posted a self-contained, minimal, reproducible example. A maintainer can copy it and run it AS IS.

### Package (Required)

- [ ] langchain
- [ ] langchain-openai
- [x] langchain-anthropic
- [ ] langchain-classic
- [ ] langchain-core
- [ ] langchain-model-profiles
- [ ] langchain-tests
- [ ] langchain-text-splitters
- [ ] langchain-chroma
- [ ] langchain-deepseek
- [ ] langchain-exa
- [ ] langchain-fireworks
- [ ] langchain-groq
- [ ] langchain-huggingface
- [ ] langchain-mistralai
- [ ] langchain-nomic
- [ ] langchain-ollama
- [ ] langchain-openrouter
- [ ] langchain-perplexity
- [ ] langchain-qdrant
- [ ] langchain-xai
- [ ] Other / not sure / general

### Related Issues / PRs

 #36288

### Reproduction Steps / Example Code (Python)

```python
# Deterministic reproduction — no API key / network needed.
# Simulates the exact event sequence Anthropic streams for an adaptive-thinking
# block whose thinking text is empty: content_block_start (thinking="") followed
# by signature_delta only, with NO thinking_delta in between.
from anthropic.types import (
    RawContentBlockDeltaEvent,
    RawContentBlockStartEvent,
    SignatureDelta,
    ThinkingBlock,
)
from langchain_anthropic.chat_models import ChatAnthropic
from langchain_core.messages import AIMessage, HumanMessage

llm = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-opus-4-6", api_key="test")

events = [
    RawContentBlockStartEvent(
        type="content_block_start",
        index=0,
        content_block=ThinkingBlock(type="thinking", thinking="", signature=""),
    ),
    RawContentBlockDeltaEvent(
        type="content_block_delta",
        index=0,
        delta=SignatureDelta(type="signature_delta", signature="EqQBCkYIBRgCIkDaq3rk..."),
    ),
]

aggregated = None
block_start_event = None
for event in events:
    chunk, block_start_event = llm._make_message_chunk_from_anthropic_event(
        event,
        stream_usage=True,
        coerce_content_to_string=False,
        block_start_event=block_start_event,
    )
    if chunk is not None:
        aggregated = chunk if aggregated is None else aggregated + chunk

print(aggregated.content)
# [{'signature': 'EqQBCkYIBRgCIkDaq3rk...', 'type': 'thinking', 'index': 0}]
#  -> the `thinking` key is MISSING

assert "thinking" not in aggregated.content[0]  # bug: passes

# Replaying this message on the next model call (e.g. agent loop after a tool
# call) produces an invalid payload that the Anthropic API rejects with 400:
payload = llm._get_request_payload(
    [HumanMessage("hi"), AIMessage(content=aggregated.content), HumanMessage("continue")]
)
print(payload["messages"][1]["content"][0])
# {'signature': 'EqQBCkYIBRgCIkDaq3rk...', 'type': 'thinking'}  -> API returns 400
```

### Error Message and Stack Trace (if applicable)

```shell
anthropic.BadRequestError: Error code: 400 - {'type': 'error', 'error': {'type': 'invalid_request_error', 'message': 'messages.1.content.0.thinking.thinking: Field required'}, 'request_id': 'req_011Ccew3oAKvavEEJnTroFjZ'}
```

### Description

* With adaptive thinking (e.g. `claude-opus-4-6`), the API can return a thinking block whose thinking text is empty — typically on the assistant turn that follows a tool result. In streaming, such a block arrives as `content_block_start` (with `thinking=""`) followed only by `signature_delta` events; no `thinking_delta` is ever emitted.
* I expect the aggregated `AIMessage` to contain a thinking block with `thinking: ""` plus the signature, so the message can be replayed on the next model call (required for tool-calling agent loops, where thinking blocks must be sent back).
* Instead, the aggregated block is `{'type': 'thinking', 'signature': '...'}` with **no `thinking` key**, and the next request fails with `400 - messages.N.content.M.thinking.thinking: Field required`. In an agent loop this makes every tool-calling conversation break on the second model call, and the corrupted message is persisted in checkpointers.

Root cause in `libs/partners/anthropic/langchain_anthropic/chat_models.py` (`_make_message_chunk_from_anthropic_event`):

1. The `content_block_start` handler added in 1.4.8 only emits the initial thinking content when `thinking or signature` is truthy. For an empty adaptive-thinking block, `content_block_start` carries `thinking=""` and `signature=""`, so nothing is emitted and the `thinking` key is lost.

```python
else:  # thinking
    thinking = getattr(event.content_block, "thinking", "") or ""
    signature = getattr(event.content_block, "signature", "") or ""
    if thinking or signature:   # <- empty block: start event is dropped entirely
        ...
```

2. The block is then reconstructed from deltas alone. A `signature_delta` produces `{'signature': ..., 'type': 'thinking'}` without a `thinking` key:

```python
elif event.delta.type in {"thinking_delta", "signature_delta"}:
    content_block = event.delta.model_dump()
    content_block["index"] = event.index
    content_block["type"] = "thinking"
    message_chunk = AIMessageChunk(content=[content_block])
```

3. On replay, `_format_messages` passes the block through unchanged (it only filters keys, it does not restore `thinking`), so the invalid block reaches the API.

Possible fixes: always emit the `content_block_start` content for thinking blocks (including `thinking=""`), or default `thinking` to `""` when building the block from a `signature_delta`. Restoring `thinking: ""` reconstructs the canonical form the API returned, so the signature still validates.

### System Info

System Information
------------------
> OS:  Darwin
> OS Version:  Darwin Kernel Version 24.3.0: Thu Jan  2 20:24:16 PST 2025; root:xnu-11215.81.4~3/RELEASE_ARM64_T6000
> Python Version:  3.12.10 (main, May 28 2025, 09:42:05) [Clang 17.0.0 (clang-1700.0.13.3)]

Package Information
-------------------

> langchain: 1.3.9

title: feat(huggingface): support Intel XPU devices in HuggingFacePipeline.from_model_id
text:
### Submission checklist

- [x] This is a feature request, not a bug report or usage question.
- [x] I added a clear and descriptive title that summarizes the feature request.
- [x] I used the GitHub search to find a similar feature request and didn't find it.
- [x] I checked the LangChain documentation and API reference to see if this feature already exists.
- [x] This is not related to the langchain-community package.

### Package (Required)

- [ ] langchain
- [ ] langchain-openai
- [ ] langchain-anthropic
- [ ] langchain-classic
- [ ] langchain-core
- [ ] langchain-model-profiles
- [ ] langchain-tests
- [ ] langchain-text-splitters
- [ ] langchain-chroma
- [ ] langchain-deepseek
- [ ] langchain-exa
- [ ] langchain-fireworks
- [ ] langchain-groq
- [x] langchain-huggingface
- [ ] langchain-mistralai
- [ ] langchain-nomic
- [ ] langchain-ollama
- [ ] langchain-openrouter
- [ ] langchain-perplexity
- [ ] langchain-qdrant
- [ ] langchain-xai
- [ ] Other / not sure / general

### Feature Description

It would be great if HuggingFacePipeline.from_model_id could run models on Intel XPU (GPU) accelerators, in addition to the existing CUDA and CPU support.

Today the device argument only accepts an integer that is validated against torch.cuda.device_count(). On a machine with an Intel XPU but no CUDA device, torch.cuda.device_count() returns 0, so any device >= 0 is rejected with a ValueError before the pipeline is ever created. There's also currently no way to target an XPU by name when both CUDA and XPU are present.

Two small enhancements would cover this:

1. An integer device (e.g. device=0) could fall back to validating against the Intel XPU device count when no CUDA device is available.
2. device could also accept a string such as "xpu:0", so users can explicitly target a specific accelerator vendor/index. A string skips the integer range-check step (which is CUDA/XPU-count based) and is forwarded as-is to the transformers pipeline; the rest of from_model_id runs unchanged. This matters most when both CUDA and XPU are available: an integer device resolves to CUDA first, so a string like "xpu:0" would be the way to direct execution onto the Intel XPU.

### Use Case

I'm running open-source models locally on Intel GPU hardware (via torch.xpu). I want to use langchain-huggingface's HuggingFacePipeline for local inference on these accelerators.

Supporting XPU here would let Intel GPU users run local models through the standard LangChain HuggingFace interface, in line with how transformers already supports XPU.

### Proposed Solution

Affected code changes are only on HuggingFacePipeline.from_model_id in libs/partners/huggingface/langchain_huggingface/llms/huggingface_pipeline.py.
- Accept int | str | None for device (currently int | None).
- When device is an integer and no CUDA device is present, count Intel XPU devices (torch.xpu.device_count(), guarded with hasattr(torch, "xpu") and torch.xpu.is_available()) and validate the range against that instead.
- When device is a string (e.g. "xpu:0"), skip the integer range check entirely and pass it directly to transformers, which already understands device strings. This is the only way to pick a vendor when both CUDA and XPU are present.
- Update the "no accelerator" warning message to be vendor-neutral (accelerator instead of GPU/CUDA).

Below is an example of how user can use after solution is implemented:
```python
from langchain_huggingface import HuggingFacePipeline

# Integer device now falls back to XPU when no CUDA device is available
llm = HuggingFacePipeline.from_model_id(
    model_id="gpt2",
    task="text-generation",
    device=0,
)

# Or target a specific accelerator by string (works when there is multiple vendor accelerators)
llm = HuggingFacePipeline.from_model_id(
    model_id="gpt2",
    task="text-generation",
    device="xpu:0",
)
```

The change is backward compatible: existing integer/CUDA behavior is unchanged, and the new string form and XPU fallback are additive.

### Alternatives Considered

Alternative approaches I have considered:

1. Use device_map="auto"

- This runs on the Intel XPU when it's the only accelerator present. But device_map is not a device selector: it hands the decision to accelerate, which distributes the model across all eligible devices rather than pinning it to one specific accelerator the user chose. So it doesn't cover the "run this on a specific XPU" case this request is about.

2. Building the transformers pipeline manually and passing it via the pipeline= argument

- This works, but it bypasses from_model_id and its convenience entirely: instead of one call, the user has to import from transformers and construct the tokenizer, model, and pipeline themselves. More prefer first-class support from LangChain directly.

### Additional Context

- transformers already supports Intel XPU devices; this brings langchain-huggingface in line with that support.
- I've put together a working implementation with unit tests and would be happy to open a PR if this direction sounds good and a maintainer is able to assign the issue to me. Open to other approaches too if you'd prefer a different design.

title: SummarizationMiddleware deletes conversation history when the summary model call fails, inserting the error text as the summary
text:
### Submission checklist

- [x] This is a bug, not a usage question.
- [x] I added a clear and descriptive title that summarizes this issue.
- [x] I used the GitHub search to find a similar question and didn't find it.
- [x] I am sure that this is a bug in LangChain rather than my code.
- [x] The bug is not resolved by updating to the latest stable version of LangChain (or the specific integration package).
- [x] This is not related to the langchain-community package.
- [x] I posted a self-contained, minimal, reproducible example. A maintainer can copy it and run it AS IS.

### Package (Required)

- [x] langchain
- [ ] langchain-openai
- [ ] langchain-anthropic
- [ ] langchain-classic
- [ ] langchain-core
- [ ] langchain-model-profiles
- [ ] langchain-tests
- [ ] langchain-text-splitters
- [ ] langchain-chroma
- [ ] langchain-deepseek
- [ ] langchain-exa
- [ ] langchain-fireworks
- [ ] langchain-groq
- [ ] langchain-huggingface
- [ ] langchain-mistralai
- [ ] langchain-nomic
- [ ] langchain-ollama
- [ ] langchain-openrouter
- [ ] langchain-perplexity
- [ ] langchain-qdrant
- [ ] langchain-xai
- [ ] Other / not sure / general

### Related Issues / PRs

* #38352 and #34794 report other SummarizationMiddleware problems, but neither covers error handling during summary generation.

### Reproduction Steps / Example Code (Python)

```python
from typing import Any

from langchain.agents.middleware.summarization import SummarizationMiddleware
from langchain_core.language_models.chat_models import BaseChatModel
from langchain_core.messages import AIMessage, HumanMessage, RemoveMessage
from langchain_core.outputs import ChatResult


class RateLimitedModel(BaseChatModel):
    """Simulates a provider that is temporarily down (e.g. 429). Never touches the network."""

    @property
    def _llm_type(self) -> str:
        return "failing-fake"

    def _generate(self, messages: Any, stop: Any = None, run_manager: Any = None, **kwargs: Any) -> ChatResult:
        raise RuntimeError("429 Too Many Requests (simulated transient failure)")


middleware = SummarizationMiddleware(
    model=RateLimitedModel(),
    trigger=("messages", 5),
    keep=("messages", 2),
    token_counter=len,
)

messages = []
for i in range(5):
    messages.append(HumanMessage(content=f"user turn {i}", id=f"h{i}"))
    messages.append(AIMessage(content=f"assistant turn {i}", id=f"a{i}"))

update = middleware.before_model({"messages": messages}, None)

print(isinstance(update["messages"][0], RemoveMessage))  # True: removes ALL messages
print(update["messages"][1].content)
# 'Here is a summary of the conversation to date:\n\nError generating summary: 429 Too Many Requests ...'
```

### Error Message and Stack Trace (if applicable)

```shell
No exception is raised, that is the problem. Observed output of the repro:

state update returned by before_model:
  RemoveMessage: ''
  HumanMessage: 'Here is a summary of the conversation to date:\n\nError generating summary: 429 Too Many Req'
  HumanMessage: 'user turn 4'
  AIMessage: 'assistant turn 4'

8 of the 10 original messages are deleted and the inserted "summary" is the provider error string.
```

### Description

When the summarization model call fails for any reason (rate limit, timeout, auth error), SummarizationMiddleware still reports success and rewrites the thread.

`_create_summary` and `_acreate_summary` catch every exception from the model call and return the string `"Error generating summary: <exception>"` as if it were a real summary (`libs/langchain_v1/langchain/agents/middleware/summarization.py`, around lines 815-822 and 841-848 on current master). `before_model` then proceeds normally: it emits `RemoveMessage(REMOVE_ALL_MESSAGES)` plus the new "summary", so the summarized messages are permanently deleted from the thread and the model is fed a summary that is literally the provider error text.

What I expect: a transient summary failure should not destroy state. Either the middleware skips summarization for that turn (keep the original messages, it can retry on a later turn) or the exception propagates so the caller can handle it.

What happens instead: see the repro. 8 of 10 messages are permanently dropped and the model continues with "Error generating summary: 429 Too Many Requests ..." as its memory of the conversation. A single 429 at the wrong moment silently corrupts a long running agent thread, and with a checkpointer the corrupted state is what gets persisted.

A contained fix would be to treat summary failure as "skip summarization this turn" (return no state update) rather than swapping real history for the error string. I have the fix and updated tests ready. I'd like to work on this, could you assign it to me?

### System Info

System Information
------------------
> OS:  Windows
> OS Version:  10.0.26200
> Python Version:  3.11.0 (main, Oct 24 2022, 18:26:48) [MSC v.1933 64 bit (AMD64)]

Package Information
-------------------
> langchain_core: 1.4.9
> langchain: 1.3.13
> langsmith: 0.10.5
> langchain_openai: 1.3.5
> langgraph_sdk: 0.4.2

Also reproduced against current master (commit 7bf8fe2), where the same except blocks are present in summarization.py.

title: Exception on _create_chat_result when the response is neither a dict or a openai.BaseModel
text:
### Submission checklist

- [x] This is a bug, not a usage question.
- [x] I added a clear and descriptive title that summarizes this issue.
- [x] I used the GitHub search to find a similar question and didn't find it.
- [x] I am sure that this is a bug in LangChain rather than my code.
- [x] The bug is not resolved by updating to the latest stable version of LangChain (or the specific integration package).

### Reproduction Steps / Example Code (Python)

```python
from langchain_openai import AzureChatOpenAI


def main() -> None:
```

```shell
        messages, stop=stop, run_manager=run_manager, **kwargs

  File "/Users/sm020124/repos/cypress/core.git/.venv/lib/python3.13/site-packages/langchain_openai/chat_models/base.py", line 1703, in _generate

```

### Description

I'm using Langchain to communicate with an OpenAI model deployed on Azure. 

When creating the client with an invalid url that returns a 302 and subsequently the redirect returns the raw string containing the html code (e.g https://azure.com), the method [_create_chat_result](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/blob/7bf8fe22163e5dadce365169e2df6b91233de9c4/libs/partners/openai/langchain_openai/chat_models/base.py#L1797) fails because it tries to call the Pydantic method `model_dump()` on a string.

This happens because the method `_create_chat_result` only accepts `dict` or `openai.BaseModel` as its parameter type (as shown in the type annotation) but it fails to take into account that the result returned by the call [response.parse()](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/blob/7bf8fe22163e5dadce365169e2df6b91233de9c4/libs/partners/openai/langchain_openai/chat_models/base.py#L1723) can return a `str` (and other types) as well.

title: bug(core): cache implementing `__len__` makes `generate`/`agenerate` raise `KeyError`
text:
### Submission checklist

- [x] This is a bug, not a usage question.
- [x] I added a clear and descriptive title that summarizes this issue.
- [x] I used the GitHub search to find a similar question and didn't find it.
- [x] I am sure that this is a bug in LangChain rather than my code.
- [x] The bug is not resolved by updating to the latest stable version of LangChain (or the specific integration package).
- [x] This is not related to the langchain-community package.
- [x] I posted a self-contained, minimal, reproducible example. A maintainer can copy it and run it AS IS.

### Package (Required)

- [ ] langchain
- [ ] langchain-openai
- [ ] langchain-anthropic
- [ ] langchain-classic
- [x] langchain-core
- [ ] langchain-model-profiles
- [ ] langchain-tests
- [ ] langchain-text-splitters
- [ ] langchain-chroma
- [ ] langchain-deepseek
- [ ] langchain-exa
- [ ] langchain-fireworks
- [ ] langchain-groq
- [ ] langchain-huggingface
- [ ] langchain-mistralai
- [ ] langchain-nomic
- [ ] langchain-ollama
- [ ] langchain-openrouter
- [ ] langchain-perplexity
- [ ] langchain-qdrant
- [ ] langchain-xai
- [ ] Other / not sure / general

### Related Issues / PRs

_No response_

### Reproduction Steps / Example Code (Python)

```python
import asyncio

from langchain_core.caches import RETURN_VAL_TYPE, BaseCache
from langchain_core.language_models import FakeListLLM


class SizedCache(BaseCache):
    """A cache that reports its entry count via __len__ (a common pattern)."""

    def __init__(self) -> None:
        self._cache: dict[tuple[str, str], RETURN_VAL_TYPE] = {}

    def lookup(self, prompt, llm_string):
        return self._cache.get((prompt, llm_string))

    def update(self, prompt, llm_string, return_val):
        self._cache[prompt, llm_string] = return_val

    def clear(self, **kwargs):
        self._cache = {}

    def __len__(self) -> int:
        return len(self._cache)


# Empty cache -> len() == 0 -> falsy
llm = FakeListLLM(cache=SizedCache(), responses=["foo", "bar"])

# Both of these raise KeyError: 0
llm.generate(["hello"])
asyncio.run(llm.agenerate(["hello"]))
```

### Error Message and Stack Trace (if applicable)

```shell
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "repro.py", line 31, in <module>
    llm.generate(["hello"])
  File ".../langchain_core/language_models/llms.py", line 1070, in generate
    generations = [existing_prompts[i] for i in range(len(prompts))]
  File ".../langchain_core/language_models/llms.py", line 1070, in <listcomp>
    generations = [existing_prompts[i] for i in range(len(prompts))]
KeyError: 0
```

### Description

The cache lookup/update helpers in `langchain_core.language_models.llms` decide whether a cache is configured using a truthiness check rather than an identity check:

- `get_prompts` (sync lookup): `if llm_cache:`
- `aget_prompts` (async lookup): `if llm_cache:`
- `aupdate_cache` (async write): `if llm_cache:`

(`update_cache`, the sync write, already correctly uses `if llm_cache is not None:`.)

In `get_prompts` / `aget_prompts` that check guards the entire lookup loop body. For a `BaseCache` that implements `__len__` (which many real caches do, to report their entry count), an empty cache is falsy, so the loop body is skipped. The prompt is then added to neither `existing_prompts` nor `missing_prompts` — it simply disappears. Generation is skipped because there are no missing prompts, and the final `generations = [existing_prompts[i] for i in range(len(prompts))]` raises `KeyError`.

This happens on both `generate` and `agenerate`, so caching is effectively unusable (it hard-crashes) for any cache that implements `__len__` and starts empty.

I expect: an empty-but-present cache should be treated as present, exactly like the non-empty case — the call should run normally and populate the cache.
Instead: `KeyError: 0`.

The fix is to use `is not None` in the three places above (matching `update_cache`). Happy to open a PR with a regression test if a maintainer can assign me.

### System Info

langchain-core==1.4.8 (also reproduces on current `main`)
Python 3.10

title: ModelRetryMiddleware and ModelFallbackMiddleware swallow `GraphInterrupt` — interrupts are retried and converted to an AIMessage instead of propagating
text:
### Submission checklist

- [x] This is a bug, not a usage question.
- [x] I added a clear and descriptive title that summarizes this issue.
- [x] I used the GitHub search to find a similar question and didn't find it.
- [x] I am sure that this is a bug in LangChain rather than my code.
- [x] The bug is not resolved by updating to the latest stable version of LangChain (or the specific integration package).
- [x] This is not related to the langchain-community package.
- [x] I posted a self-contained, minimal, reproducible example. A maintainer can copy it and run it AS IS.

### Package (Required)

- [x] langchain
- [ ] langchain-openai
- [ ] langchain-anthropic
- [ ] langchain-classic
- [ ] langchain-core
- [ ] langchain-model-profiles
- [ ] langchain-tests
- [ ] langchain-text-splitters
- [ ] langchain-chroma
- [ ] langchain-deepseek
- [ ] langchain-exa
- [ ] langchain-fireworks
- [ ] langchain-groq
- [ ] langchain-huggingface
- [ ] langchain-mistralai
- [ ] langchain-nomic
- [ ] langchain-ollama
- [ ] langchain-openrouter
- [ ] langchain-perplexity
- [ ] langchain-qdrant
- [ ] langchain-xai
- [ ] Other / not sure / general

### Related Issues / PRs

#38722 fixed exactly this for `ToolRetryMiddleware` ("Control-flow signals (interrupts, parent commands) must propagate, not be retried or converted to error messages") — the model-side retry/fallback middleware never received the same guard.

Supersedes #38836, which was auto-closed by the triage automation because the package selection did not register when the form was submitted; re-filed with the section completed.

### Reproduction Steps / Example Code (Python)

```python
"""No network needed. An inner middleware (or handler) raising GraphInterrupt —
which is what langgraph's interrupt() does — is retried and then converted
into a plain AIMessage by ModelRetryMiddleware."""

from langchain.agents.middleware.model_retry import ModelRetryMiddleware
from langchain.agents.middleware.types import ModelRequest
from langchain_core.language_models.fake_chat_models import GenericFakeChatModel
from langgraph.errors import GraphBubbleUp, GraphInterrupt

print("GraphInterrupt is Exception subclass:", issubclass(GraphInterrupt, Exception))
print("GraphInterrupt is GraphBubbleUp subclass:", issubclass(GraphInterrupt, GraphBubbleUp))

model = GenericFakeChatModel(messages=iter([]))
request = ModelRequest(model=model, messages=[])

mw = ModelRetryMiddleware(max_retries=2, initial_delay=0.0, jitter=False)

calls = {"n": 0}


def handler(req):
    # Stands in for an inner wrap_model_call middleware calling interrupt(),
    # e.g. an approval gate before an expensive model call.
    calls["n"] += 1
    raise GraphInterrupt()


result = mw.wrap_model_call(request, handler)
print("handler invocations:", calls["n"])
print("returned instead of propagating interrupt:", type(result).__name__)
print("AIMessage content:", result.result[0].content)
```

### Error Message and Stack Trace (if applicable)

```shell
# No exception is raised — that is the bug. Actual output:

GraphInterrupt is Exception subclass: True
GraphInterrupt is GraphBubbleUp subclass: True
handler invocations: 3
returned instead of propagating interrupt: ModelResponse
AIMessage content: Model call failed after 3 attempts with GraphInterrupt: ()

# expected: GraphInterrupt propagates out of wrap_model_call on the first raise
# (as ToolRetryMiddleware does since #38722)
```

### Description

`ModelRetryMiddleware.wrap_model_call` / `awrap_model_call` (`langchain/agents/middleware/model_retry.py`) catch bare `Exception` around `handler(request)`. `GraphInterrupt` — what `langgraph.types.interrupt()` raises — is an `Exception` subclass, so when any middleware *inner* to the retry middleware in the `wrap_model_call` chain interrupts (a human-approval gate before expensive model calls is the natural example), the interrupt is:

1. "retried" `max_retries` times, with backoff sleeps in between (the repro shows 3 handler invocations for `max_retries=2`), and then
2. with the default `on_failure="continue"`, converted into a plain `AIMessage` — so the run **completes normally instead of pausing**. The interrupt, and with it the human-in-the-loop pause, is silently destroyed. With `on_failure="error"` it surfaces as a `ModelRetryLimitExceededError` instead of an interrupt, which is equally wrong.

`ModelFallbackMiddleware.wrap_model_call` / `awrap_model_call` (`model_fallback.py`) have the same catch-all: an interrupt raised beneath it is treated as a model failure and the middleware pointlessly walks the entire fallback chain, re-invoking the interrupt once per fallback model. (The same catch-alls also swallow `GraphBubbleUp`-based escalations generally, e.g. parent `Command` signals.)

This is precisely the defect class fixed for the tool side in #38722, which added

```python
except GraphBubbleUp:
    # Control-flow signals (interrupts, parent commands) must
    # propagate, not be retried or converted to error messages.
    raise
```

before the generic handler in `ToolRetryMiddleware` (both sync and async paths). The model-side middleware needs the same guard in the four places (`ModelRetryMiddleware.wrap_model_call`/`awrap_model_call`, `ModelFallbackMiddleware.wrap_model_call`/`awrap_model_call`).

Expected: `GraphInterrupt`/`GraphBubbleUp` propagate immediately, exactly as they do through `ToolRetryMiddleware` since #38722.

Verified on the released `langchain 1.3.13` (with `langgraph 1.2.9`) and unchanged on current `master` (local editable install; `model_retry.py` and `model_fallback.py` catch bare `Exception` with no `GraphBubbleUp` guard).

I'd like to work on this — the fix mirrors #38722, and I'd add sync+async unit tests for both middleware asserting that a handler raising `GraphInterrupt` propagates on the first call (no retries, no fallback traversal, no `AIMessage` conversion). Could you assign me?

### System Info

System Information
------------------
> OS:  Windows
> OS Version:  10.0.26200
> Python Version:  3.12.0 (tags/v3.12.0:0fb18b0, Oct  2 2023, 13:03:39) [MSC v.1935 64 bit (AMD64)]

Package Information
-------------------
> langchain_core: 1.4.9
> langchain: 1.3.13
> langsmith: 0.8.18
> langchain_openai: 1.3.5
> langgraph_sdk: 0.4.2

Other Dependencies
------------------
> langgraph: 1.2.9
> pydantic: 2.12.5

title: HumanInTheLoopMiddleware silently auto-approves tools whose `InterruptOnConfig` is invalid (typo'd or missing `allowed_decisions`)
text:
### Submission checklist

- [x] This is a bug, not a usage question.
- [x] I added a clear and descriptive title that summarizes this issue.
- [x] I used the GitHub search to find a similar question and didn't find it.
- [x] I am sure that this is a bug in LangChain rather than my code.
- [x] The bug is not resolved by updating to the latest stable version of LangChain (or the specific integration package).
- [x] This is not related to the langchain-community package.
- [x] I posted a self-contained, minimal, reproducible example. A maintainer can copy it and run it AS IS.

### Package (Required)

- [x] langchain
- [ ] langchain-openai
- [ ] langchain-anthropic
- [ ] langchain-classic
- [ ] langchain-core
- [ ] langchain-model-profiles
- [ ] langchain-tests
- [ ] langchain-text-splitters
- [ ] langchain-chroma
- [ ] langchain-deepseek
- [ ] langchain-exa
- [ ] langchain-fireworks
- [ ] langchain-groq
- [ ] langchain-huggingface
- [ ] langchain-mistralai
- [ ] langchain-nomic
- [ ] langchain-ollama
- [ ] langchain-openrouter
- [ ] langchain-perplexity
- [ ] langchain-qdrant
- [ ] langchain-xai
- [ ] Other / not sure / general

### Related Issues / PRs

Adjacent in spirit to #38718 (docs using wrong field names for PII custom detectors) — TypedDict-based configs give no runtime protection against key typos, and here the failure mode is a disabled safety gate.

### Reproduction Steps / Example Code (Python)

```python
"""No network needed: an invalid InterruptOnConfig disables the approval gate
without any error or warning."""

from langchain.agents.middleware.human_in_the_loop import HumanInTheLoopMiddleware
from langchain_core.messages import AIMessage, HumanMessage

# Typo: "allowed_decision" instead of "allowed_decisions"
mw = HumanInTheLoopMiddleware(
    interrupt_on={"delete_database": {"allowed_decision": ["approve"]}}
)
print("resolved interrupt_on:", mw.interrupt_on)

ai = AIMessage(
    content="",
    tool_calls=[{"name": "delete_database", "args": {"db": "prod"}, "id": "c1"}],
)
state = {"messages": [HumanMessage("wipe prod"), ai]}
result = mw.after_model(state, None)  # would call interrupt() if config were honored
print("after_model result:", result)

# A config carrying only a `when` predicate (no allowed_decisions) is dropped too:
mw2 = HumanInTheLoopMiddleware(interrupt_on={"delete_database": {"when": lambda req: True}})
print("resolved interrupt_on (when-only config):", mw2.interrupt_on)
```

### Error Message and Stack Trace (if applicable)

```shell
# No exception, no warning — that is the bug. Actual output:

resolved interrupt_on: {}
after_model result: None
resolved interrupt_on (when-only config): {}

# after_model returning None means the delete_database call proceeds
# with no interrupt/approval
```

### Description

`HumanInTheLoopMiddleware.__init__` (`langchain/agents/middleware/human_in_the_loop.py`) resolves the `interrupt_on` mapping like this:

```python
for tool_name, tool_config in interrupt_on.items():
    if isinstance(tool_config, bool):
        if tool_config is True:
            resolved_configs[tool_name] = InterruptOnConfig(...)
    elif tool_config.get("allowed_decisions"):
        resolved_configs[tool_name] = tool_config
    # else: entry silently dropped -> tool auto-approved
```

`InterruptOnConfig` is a `TypedDict`, so nothing validates keys at runtime. Any mapping that doesn't contain a truthy `allowed_decisions` — a typo'd key (`allowed_decision`), an empty list, or a config carrying only `description` / `when` — is **silently discarded**, and the tool the user explicitly listed for human approval executes with no interrupt, no error, and no warning.

For a safety middleware this is the worst possible failure direction: the user's config says "gate `delete_database` behind a human", and a one-character typo turns that into "run `delete_database` unattended". Only `False` is documented as auto-approve; everything else under `interrupt_on` expresses intent to gate.

Expected: an invalid `InterruptOnConfig` raises `ValueError` at construction time (mapping present but `allowed_decisions` missing/empty — and ideally unknown keys rejected as well), so misconfiguration fails loudly instead of disabling the gate.

Verified on the released `langchain 1.3.13` and unchanged on current `master` (local editable install).

I'd like to work on this: validation in `__init__` raising `ValueError` with the offending tool name and key(s), plus unit tests covering the typo'd key, empty `allowed_decisions`, `when`-only config, and the valid/bool paths staying intact. Since this turns silent acceptance into an exception it's technically behavior-tightening — happy to make it a warning instead if maintainers prefer. Could you assign me?

### System Info

System Information
------------------
> OS:  Windows
> OS Version:  10.0.26200
> Python Version:  3.12.0 (tags/v3.12.0:0fb18b0, Oct  2 2023, 13:03:39) [MSC v.1935 64 bit (AMD64)]

Package Information
-------------------
> langchain_core: 1.4.9
> langchain: 1.3.13
> langsmith: 0.8.18
> langchain_openai: 1.3.5
> langgraph_sdk: 0.4.2

Other Dependencies
------------------
> langgraph: 1.2.9
> pydantic: 2.12.5

title: Add TokenBudgetMiddleware for token-usage budgets in agents
text:
### Submission checklist

- [x] This is a feature request, not a bug report or usage question.
- [x] I added a clear and descriptive title that summarizes the feature request.
- [x] I used the GitHub search to find a similar feature request and didn't find it.
- [x] I checked the LangChain documentation and API reference to see if this feature already exists.
- [x] This is not related to the langchain-community package.

### Package (Required)

- [x] langchain
- [ ] langchain-openai
- [ ] langchain-anthropic
- [ ] langchain-classic
- [ ] langchain-core
- [ ] langchain-model-profiles
- [ ] langchain-tests
- [ ] langchain-text-splitters
- [ ] langchain-chroma
- [ ] langchain-deepseek
- [ ] langchain-exa
- [ ] langchain-fireworks
- [ ] langchain-groq
- [ ] langchain-huggingface
- [ ] langchain-mistralai
- [ ] langchain-nomic
- [ ] langchain-ollama
- [ ] langchain-openrouter
- [ ] langchain-perplexity
- [ ] langchain-qdrant
- [ ] langchain-xai
- [ ] Other / not sure / general

### Feature Description

LangChain's agent middleware suite can cap the *number* of model calls (`ModelCallLimitMiddleware`) and tool calls (`ToolCallLimitMiddleware`), but nothing bounds *token consumption*. I'd like a `TokenBudgetMiddleware` that accumulates token usage across model calls (from `AIMessage.usage_metadata`) and halts the agent — via `jump_to="end"` or a `TokenBudgetExceededError` — when a configured budget is exceeded. It would support independent input/output/total limits at both thread and run scope, with sync and async hooks, mirroring `ModelCallLimitMiddleware`'s structure and `exit_behavior` contract.

### Use Case

Production agents can consume a very large token volume within a small number of calls (context grows with tool outputs and retrieved documents), so a call-count cap doesn't bound spend or context blowup. `SummarizationMiddleware` and `ContextEditingMiddleware` reduce context reactively but don't enforce a hard ceiling. Today users hand-roll `before_model` / `after_model` hooks re-reading `usage_metadata` and managing state manually. A reusable, provider-agnostic budget guard would cover a common production need.

### Proposed Solution

```python
from langchain.agents.middleware import TokenBudgetMiddleware
from langchain.agents import create_agent

budget = TokenBudgetMiddleware(
    thread_total_limit=200_000,
    run_total_limit=50_000,
    exit_behavior="end",  # or "error"
)
agent = create_agent("openai:gpt-5.5", middleware=[budget])
```

- `after_model` reads `usage_metadata` off the latest `AIMessage` and accumulates input/output/total tokens for thread + run scope (defensive: missing usage = zero contribution, never raises).
- `before_model` (with `@hook_config(can_jump_to=["end"])`) checks accumulated usage against any set limit; on breach it injects an explanatory `AIMessage` and jumps to end, or raises `TokenBudgetExceededError`, per `exit_behavior`.
- State extends `AgentState` with private token-count fields, reusing the same `PrivateStateAttr` / `UntrackedValue` channel annotations `ModelCallLimitMiddleware` uses for thread- vs run-scoped persistence.
- Scope is token-only; monetary cost (which needs drifting per-model pricing tables) is left as a possible follow-up.

**Test cases that should pass**

- Under budget → no action; accumulation is additive across calls.
- Thread/run total limit reached → `jump_to="end"` with a message naming the breached limit; `error` mode raises `TokenBudgetExceededError`.
- Independent input-only and output-only limit breaches trigger correctly.
- Missing / `None` `usage_metadata` accumulates nothing and never raises.
- Async hooks match sync behavior; thread scope persists across invocations while run scope resets (via `create_agent` + checkpointer).
- Constructor with all-`None` limits or invalid `exit_behavior` raises `ValueError`.

### Alternatives Considered

- Extending `SummarizationMiddleware` / `ContextEditingMiddleware` — these are reactive context reducers, not enforceable budget guards; conflating the two would muddy their purpose.
- A user-maintained custom hook — works (and is a fine stopgap today), but is boilerplate every team re-implements; consumption limiting belongs alongside the existing call-count limiters.

### Additional Context

Related: #35766 (proactive `ToolCallLimitMiddleware`). Prior PRs #35381 / #35751 / #35753 attempted this and were closed without maintainer comment — consistent with the issue-first process (unassigned PRs), not a technical rejection. I'd follow the assign-first flow before opening any PR.

This was first posted to the forum for feedback, where a LangChain team member confirmed it addresses a real gap, that the `before_model` + `@hook_config(can_jump_to=["end"])` + accumulated `usage_metadata` pattern is the right approach, and directed me to file it here as a feature request.

title: AddableDict silently drops data on type-incompatible merge (langchain_core.runnables.utils)
text:
### Submission checklist

- [x] This is a bug, not a usage question.
- [x] I added a clear and descriptive title that summarizes this issue.
- [x] I used the GitHub search to find a similar question and didn't find it.
- [x] I am sure that this is a bug in LangChain rather than my code.
- [x] The bug is not resolved by updating to the latest stable version of LangChain (or the specific integration package).
- [x] This is not related to the langchain-community package.
- [x] I posted a self-contained, minimal, reproducible example. A maintainer can copy it and run it AS IS.

### Package (Required)

- [ ] langchain
- [ ] langchain-openai
- [ ] langchain-anthropic
- [ ] langchain-classic
- [x] langchain-core
- [ ] langchain-model-profiles
- [ ] langchain-tests
- [ ] langchain-text-splitters
- [ ] langchain-chroma
- [ ] langchain-deepseek
- [ ] langchain-exa
- [ ] langchain-fireworks
- [ ] langchain-groq
- [ ] langchain-huggingface
- [ ] langchain-mistralai
- [ ] langchain-nomic
- [ ] langchain-ollama
- [ ] langchain-openrouter
- [ ] langchain-perplexity
- [ ] langchain-qdrant
- [ ] langchain-xai
- [ ] Other / not sure / general

### Related Issues / PRs

_No response_

### Reproduction Steps / Example Code (Python)

```python
from langchain_core.runnables.utils import AddableDict

a = AddableDict({"count": 1})
b = AddableDict({"count": "some_string"})
result = a + b
print(result)
```

### Error Message and Stack Trace (if applicable)

```shell
No error is raised. This is the bug — the operation should either succeed correctly or raise a clear error, but instead it silently produces incorrect output with no indication anything went wrong.
```

### Description

I'm using AddableDict (langchain_core.runnables.utils), which is used internally 
in RunnablePassthrough and LCEL's streaming/chunk-aggregation path.

I expect merging two AddableDict instances with a type-incompatible value at the 
same key to either merge correctly or raise a clear error.

Instead, __add__ and __radd__ catch TypeError internally and silently fall back 
to the right-hand value, discarding the left-hand value with no warning:

    try:
        added = chunk[key] + other[key]
    except TypeError:
        added = other[key]
    chunk[key] = added

In the reproduction above, the int 1 is silently lost and replaced by the string, 
with no exception or log. Since AddableDict is used in real chain output 
aggregation, this could cause silent data loss whenever streamed/merged chunks 
have mismatched types at the same key — a caller has no way to know data was dropped.

Suggested fix: raise a clear error instead of silently discarding data, e.g.:

    except TypeError as e:
        raise TypeError(
            f"Cannot add incompatible types for key '{key}': "
            f"{type(chunk[key])} and {type(other[key])}"
        ) from e
